metadata.seed: I just feel like such a failure. It makes me just want to curl up, and I just feel so useless. I just feel like everything I touch, I'm doing it the wrong way, and it's making things worse instead of better.

patient.query: I just feel like such a failure. It makes me just want to curl up, and I just feel so useless. I just feel like everything I touch, I'm doing it the wrong way, and it's making things worse instead of better.

automatic_thoughts: I feel like such a failure
I feel so useless
I'm doing everything the wrong way
It's making things worse instead of better

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense that everything you do is falling short. What moments have made you feel like you’re doing things the wrong way? Can you think of any times when something you tried actually helped or improved a situation?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel like that sometimes. It’s like no matter what I do, it just never feels good enough. I remember trying to help a friend through a tough time, but I ended up feeling like I made it worse. On the flip side, there was one time when I spoke up in a group project, and it felt good to share my ideas, even though I wasn’t sure at first if they were any good. I just… wish that kind of feeling happened

triggers: speaking up in a group project
automatic_thoughts: no matter what I do, it just never feels good enough
I made it worse
I wasn't sure if my ideas were any good
I wish that kind of feeling happened

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you sometimes feel your help falls short, especially when you’re trying to support a friend. What signs or feedback do you notice that make you think your efforts aren’t enough? Can you think of moments when your contributions were appreciated or made a difference, even if it was small?

patient.query: Yeah, it's hard to shake that feeling. Like, when my friend didn't seem better after I talked to them, I felt like I had failed somehow. Or if they didn’t thank me, it makes me think, “Maybe they didn’t find what I said helpful at all.” But, I guess there was that time when I helped my coworker with a presentation, and they said it really made a difference for them. It felt nice to hear, but then I wonder if it was just

automatic_thoughts: I had failed somehow
Maybe they didn’t find what I said helpful at all
it was just a coincidence

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing how the reactions of others shape how you view yourself. What evidence do you have that your help was or wasn’t helpful, and how might other factors have influenced their response? How might you view the situation if you considered that their reaction could be about something else entirely?

patient.query: I suppose I only really focus on their reactions. Like, if someone seems indifferent, I take it personally and think I messed up. But maybe they were just having a bad day or dealing with their own stuff. I don't know, it’s just hard not to feel like their response is a direct reflection of me. It’s like my worth flops around based on what other people think or feel, and that gets exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I messed up
their response is a direct reflection of me
my worth flops around based on what others think

neutral: Ability to understand own emotions (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel your worth shifts with how others respond. You might consider that your value comes from your own actions and qualities, not just how people react. What are some things you value about yourself that don’t depend on external feedback?

patient.query: I suppose I really focus on their reactions. If someone seems indifferent, I take it personally and think I messed up. Maybe they were just having a bad day or dealing with their own stuff. But it’s hard not to feel like their response is a direct reflection of me. My worth feels like it flops around based on what other people think or feel, and that gets exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I messed up
their response is a direct reflection of me
my worth flops around based on what others think

neutral: Ability to understand own emotions (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of exhaustion from interpreting others’ reactions as a reflection of your worth. What evidence do you have that their indifference is really about you? Are there other possible reasons they might be acting that way?

patient.query: I just can’t help but focus on how they react, you know? If someone seems indifferent, I immediately feel like I messed up. It makes me wonder if I'm just not good enough, or if I said the wrong thing. I know they might be dealing with their own stuff, but it still feels so personal. It’s like my worth is tied to how others see me, and that just wears me out.

automatic_thoughts: I messed up
I'm not good enough
I said the wrong thing
my worth is tied to how others see me

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how exhausting it feels to tie your worth to how others react. What evidence do you have that their indifference means you messed up? Could there be another way to interpret their reaction that doesn’t reflect on your value?

patient.query: I feel like I always focus on how others react. If someone seems indifferent, I can’t help but think I messed up. It makes me wonder if I’m just not good enough or if I said the wrong thing. Even though I know they might be going through their own stuff, it still hits me hard. It feels like my worth is tied to how others see me, and that just wears me out.

automatic_thoughts: I messed up
I'm not good enough
I said the wrong thing
my worth is tied to how others see me

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure about how others see you. What evidence do you have that your worth is tied to their reactions? How might you view the situation if you considered that their indifference could be about them, not you?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like a lot sometimes. I guess I don’t really have solid evidence; it’s more like a gut feeling that if I’m not getting positive feedback, then I must not be good enough. When I think about it, maybe their indifference really is about their own stuff. But it’s hard to separate that when it feels so personal in the moment. I want to be strong enough to not let their reactions get to me, but it just feels so heavy sometimes.

automatic_thoughts: I must not be good enough
it feels personal
I want to be strong enough

entailment: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the lack of feedback is weighing heavily on you. What evidence do you have that this lack of feedback reflects your worth? How might you consider other explanations for their indifference?

patient.query: I guess I don’t really have solid proof. It’s more about how I feel in those moments. I think I just get so caught up in my own head. Maybe they’re distracted or busy, and it isn’t about me at all, but it feels like it is when I’m just standing there waiting for a response. Still, all those thoughts just swirl around and make it hard to see anything clearly. It’s like I want to be strong enough to brush it off, but it

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have solid proof
it feels like it is about me
thoughts swirl around
I want to be strong enough to brush it off

neutral: Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)
Ability to understand own emotions (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a loop of thoughts that make it hard to see what’s really happening. You notice that when you’re waiting for a reply, it feels personal even if you suspect it’s not. Trying to stay strong while those thoughts swirl can feel exhausting. You’re not alone in feeling that way, and it’s understandable that it feels overwhelming.

patient.query: Yeah, that’s exactly it. I just get trapped in my own thoughts, and it makes everything feel so heavy. I know my mind is probably exaggerating things, but it’s hard to break free from that feeling when it happens. I really want to feel more confident and not let those moments define me, but it just feels like such a struggle. Sometimes I’d rather just avoid situations altogether than deal with that anxiety.

automatic_thoughts: my mind is exaggerating things
I can't break free from this feeling
I want to feel more confident
those moments define me

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those thoughts feel overwhelming when they arise. What evidence do you have that your mind is exaggerating, and what evidence might suggest it’s not? How could you test a different way of looking at those moments? What small step might you try that could help you feel a bit more confident without feeling like you’re facing the whole anxiety head‑on?
